pullRequest.title: [DOM-75323] Start and Stop Endpoints for App Versions

nodes.body:
This method currently does not take input for imported git repositories/ imported projects.
Changes around that will go into a separate PR since this one's already getting complicated.
I'm not sure what the behavior should be here.

i guess if we're making some of these non-optional like i suggest elsewhere, then we need to resolve them first.

if not, then we have to think through the implications of allowing `None` here for things like commitId

question: in various places here we're now deriving version and app from instance. What would happen in the case where we wouldn't have an instance? It would be unusual but possible (app created but never got to launch for some reason)
I'll add a fallback to the previous behavior to get the latest numerical app version when there are no instances to be fetched for an app.

question: these can now return empty for existing apps, I think. Do you expect this to be covered via migration or we should have some level of backwards compat here to avoid precise frontend syncing?
Most of these are already in the app version, but we definitely want a migration to handle the git ref and dfs commit.
question: what is the idea for the behaviour for other types, non app types? Should we explicitly restrict if we want to instead of silently returning?

This would never be called with anything that isn't an app (and even if it did, either the version call will fail or the check that the version belongs to the modelproduct will fail).

I can simplify to remove the silent failure cases.
note: it seems like instanceId was never really used. Could we take it out from the method signature all the way?

Hm... taking a closer look at this, seems like the UI is using this as a generic stop app API endpoint.

Preferably I would want to make this match the described behavior and only delete that app instance if it was running, but I'm not sure if it would break existing behavior.
question: do we need to set the current version in the app object? or it's already happening and I missed it? :D
`createAppVersion` already does it. `creationOrder` is the field storing the version number.
nit clarification: i believe `creationOrder` is a monotonic counter that isnt guaranteed to be what is in the UI: i believe the "version number" shown in the UI comes from the ordering.
We do in the controller.

suggestion: maybe we need to make the error message a little more clear and/or user friendly if it's going to be shown in the UI
it's possible users will try to stop a version already stopped so perhaps saying something like "this app version is not running or is invalid" or something of sorts, might be helpful

question: the existing `publishAppVersion` does a lot of other things before starting the app. I like how this is implemented here but just want to mention it in case some important step was missing. 
The only thing I see is the usage of `hasIngress` but there's a comment suggestion we don't need that anymore, in fact.
Otherwise it doesn't seem to me we've missed anything important
Good catch, there's a guardrails check I'm missing in startVersion that is in start (in InProcessModelProductService). I can add that back in.

did you consider the performance implications of this?
- this returns every single instance ever for the app
- then we sort and take a single one

and this method is going to execute once per app for some endpoints like `list apps`, right?

is there some better query or pattern we can use further down the stack? like doing the sorting and "take one" on mongo side?

or even a special-purpose `"getLatestInstances(appIds: Seq[id])"` on the instance persister (run persister) so we remove the N+1 query pattern worry?

Makes sense to do the sort on the mongo side.
Batching/ joining the persister queries is also fairly doable here.
i see we have another ticket to address -- cool

many of these args are an `Option`. example: compute env revision.

did you think about making the now-non-optional fields of an app version -- like compute env revisions -- _actually_ non optional in the `createAppVersion` and also in the `case class AppVersion` itself? 

right now it's not clear to me what is actually optional. i would have thought we're making the non-optional things not optional (of course, we need a migration to put in "resolved" fields to past versions)

I'm a bit hesitant to make fields not optional. We have a weird edge case with project templates that have apps where we create a dummy version with most of the fields intentionally empty (since we can't check the template itself/ new projects using the template may not necessarily have access to the environment/ hardware tier the original app does).

We do need some reasonable fallback behavior for starting an app with empty fields. I'm leaning towards preventing restarts of such versions (should be minimal after a migration), but let me know if you have other ideas.

"leaning towards preventing restarts of such version"

makes sense to me: `case class AppVersion()` can have some optional fields to work with weird edge cases, but such versions cannot actually be "launched"/ran.

how about the new `launchVersion()` method declares the actual non-optional fields (doesnt have to be within that method, could even be some method/set/list on the AppVersion companion object for example), and validates they are present, throwing if there is some missing required field?
EDIT -- i misread
oops, i misread -- this works, please disregard!
can you put a comment. something like: `// this assumes there is only one running version of the app. will have to change if/when we relax this assumption`
(when that time comes we could easily write a `findActiveAppVersionRun()` method and the case statement below is not needed)
is a commit id always included here?
looking at the tests... maybe it does? but would it be clearer to included if `dto.type != commitId then throw` or some other way to make this more clear that it is always "commitId"?
There's a logger warn if we are unable to resolve a specific commit, but makes sense to just throw an error if we are unable to resolve the commit at while the app version is being created.

just a heads up, we have some performance issues with app endpoints. i think this `listApps()` endpoint in particular can get very slow if there are 100+ apps.

as customers use apps more and more, this problem will only get worse, and be a big pain point.

at some point -- hopefully before 6.3 -- we may need to do a serious refactor of at least this controller method's callstack.

i believe right now we're basically querying for all apps, sorting and limiting/paging in scala memory, then doing these N+1 queries to get versions and instances.

it will take some thought and effort on how to refactor this. example: push _all_ sorting/paging logic down into one persister method db query to do that db side instead of scala in-memory, and also do the lookups/joins app => app_version => app_instance in the same query pipeline to do _that_ db side instead of N+1 query pattern.

one step at a time though -- if for example sorting/paging db side takes care of a lot of performance issue, can defer this N+1 query problem.
cc @ddl-bira-ignacio so you are aware of this issue
Addressing these in a follow-up PR here: https://github.com/cerebrotech/domino/pull/47229

yea just re-iterating bira's point: if a version was created but no instance created from that (actually not a super unlikely scenario), then we'll get no version.

im not sure what the best fallback is:
- fallback to latest version
- fallback to latest version that has an instance
- fallback to latest version that has the latest-run instance

all those could be different

I'm currently using

fallback to any version that has the latest-run instance
fallback to latest (numerical) version if no instances exist

which seems to make the most sense in this context.
sounds good
this looks odd. `version.commitId` has a field `.name`?

The field wants to return a string. 
If I'm not mistaken, this should be the correct pattern for getting the commit hash from the commitId object.
gotcha, i didn't realize commitId was an object with child fields
FYI https://archive.eclipse.org/jgit/docs/jgit-2.0.0.201206130900-r/apidocs/org/eclipse/jgit/lib/AnyObjectId.html#name()
im glad we can get rid of this monstrosity!

can we add a test that starting an "older" version stops a running "newer" version?

that's new allowed behavior that i think is helpful to codify in a test
why add this to the instance (run)?
This was accidental. Removing it.

this should throw for any part of the app version that is undefined but (previously, before this work) resolved at runtime. we took care of commit id, yes, but there are others: environment id, environment revision id can be None. these are resolved at runtime by looking at the project default, right? that could change over time.

to ensure "reproducible" versions, all such fields like this must receive the same "resolve" treatment if allowed to be None at any point -- need to resolve and persist the resolved thing before launching.

(and by the way, need to prevent "update unmodifiable field foo like commitId/env rev id/etc" while allowing "update modifiable field like hwt" -- we chatted about that before, to be taken care of in follow on work)
im not sure what gaps there are here, but i think MDLC team should fully understand this point, and take care of it in a follow up PR is OK with me

we have gaps that matt b is aware of that are harder to close. like "mount no datasets" vs "mount all" -- set of "all" can change over time.

we don't know what to do about that yet. for now, it is good enough that everyone is aware of these limitations.
related https://github.com/cerebrotech/domino/pull/47189#discussion_r2941815314

I'll add guards for environmentId and environmentRevisionId for now. They generally should have those in the app version, but there's an edge-case where pre-2019 apps didn't have them defined, blocking restarts of those apps seems reasonable.
